content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Miki Vohryzek-Bolden 
Executive Director 
California Association of 
Councils of Governments 
P.O. Box 808 
Sacramento, CA 
95804 
Dear Ms. Vohryzek-Bolden: 
September 23, 1988 
Re: 
88-366 
Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform 
Act was received on September 22, 1988 by the Fair Political 
Practices commission. 
If you have any questions about your 
advice request, you may contact John McLean, an attorney in the 
Legal Division, directly at (916) 322-5901. 
We try to answer all advice requests promptly. 
Therefore, 
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, 
or more information is needed, you should expect a response 
within 21 working days if your request seeks formal written 
advice. 
If more information is needed, the person assigned to 
prepare a response to your request will contact you shortly to 
advise you as to information needed. 
If your request is for 
informal assistance, we will answer it as quickly as we can. 
(See Commission Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 
18329).) 
You also should be aware that your letter and our response 
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon 
receipt of a proper request for disclosure. 
DMG:plh 
Very truly yours, 
[A'~i I.

COUNCILS OF GOVERNMENTS 
John McLean 
Staff Attorney 
Diversity and Unity 
September 21, 1988 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
P.O. Box 807 
Sacramento, CA 
95804 
Dear John: 
I am writing to you to request some information on the effects 
of Proposition 73 on the content of our newsletter. 
CALCOG is a 
state association composed of approximately 25 regional councils 
of governments (COGs). 
The association receives dues from these 
COGs to support is operation. 
The dues are public moneys. 
I would like you to answer the following question: 
Can our quarterly newsletter include, for instance, an article 
on the new officers for the association, if the newsletter is 
mailed to these 25 COGs plus approximately 25 other indivi-
duals? 
In other words, would the mailing to the COGs be 
considered "intra-agency" and therefore not be included within 
the definition of "1egis1ative newsletter or other mass mai1ing" 
(Government Code Section 89001)? 
Please call me if you have any questions about this request. 
look forward to hearing from you. 
Sincerely, 
I 
~):/~ 
~ I / (y(_/u:y:f ~&'G('~

MIKI VOHRYZEK-BOLDE~ ~ 
EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR 


State of California 
Memorandum 
To 
:File 
Date 
october 12, 1988 
From 
: 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
John G. McLean 
Subject :Advice Request No. T-88-366 
Spoke today with Miki Vohryzek-Bolden. 
Advised her that 
mass mailing provisions apply only to mailings of more than 200 
pieces. 
Also advised her that mailings which consist only of 
mailings sent in the normal course of business from one 
governmental entity or officer to another governmental entity 
or officer are not prohibited. 
Sent her an advice letter 
(I-88-275) which I felt addressed some of the concerns she was 
raising. 
JGM:ld:T88366

sections.question: Can our quarterly newsletter include, for instance, an article on the new officers for the association, if the newsletter is mailed to these 25 COGs plus approximately 25 other indivi-duals? In other words, would the mailing to the COGs be considered "intra-agency" and therefore not be included within the definition of "legislative newsletter or other mass mailing" (Government Code Section 89001)?
sections.question_synthetic: Whether CALCOG's quarterly newsletter mailing to member COGs and other individuals would constitute a 'legislative newsletter or other mass mailing' under Government Code Section 89001, or whether the intra-agency mailings would be exempt from Proposition 73 requirements.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains three related pieces: (1) incoming request letter from CALCOG executive director; (2) FPPC acknowledgment letter dated September 23, 1988; (3) internal memorandum dated October 12, 1988 documenting informal advice provided. No formal written advice letter or conclusion section found in these documents.

embedding.summary: This document contains an incoming advice request from the California Association of Councils of Governments regarding whether Proposition 73's mass mailing restrictions apply to their quarterly newsletter. It includes the initial FPPC acknowledgment letter and a follow-up internal memorandum documenting informal telephone advice provided by staff attorney John McLean.
